abstract:
This review examines the pivotal role of gas-phase transition metal clusters as tractable models for understanding surface chemistry and heterogeneous catalysis at the atomic level. Central to this field has been the evolution of size-specific experimental methodologies—most notably infrared (IR) action spectroscopy—which allow for the structural characterization of isolated clusters and their complexes in a vacuum, free from substrate or solvent interference. By utilizing high-intensity, widely tunable light sources such as infrared free-electron lasers (IR-FELs), researchers can now probe the vibrational signatures of molecular ligands to decipher their binding configurations, bond activation mechanisms, and subsequent chemical transformations. 

A comprehensive analysis is provided for complexes involving a variety of small molecules, with a detailed focus on the binding and activation of H$_2$, CO, N$_2$, and O$_2$, as well as triatomic and larger organic ligands. The review highlights how cluster size, charge state, and geometry influence reactivity, often revealing non-monotonic properties where "every atom counts." Furthermore, it discusses the phenomenon of IR-induced reactions—such as dehydrogenation or CO oxidation—where the cluster acts as a nanoscale laboratory for elementary catalytic steps. By bridging the gap between molecular coordination chemistry and bulk surface science, this work underscores how vibrational spectroscopy of gas-phase clusters provides critical quantitative benchmarks for modern quantum chemical theories and the design of advanced nanocatalysts.
